claim: An agent is a system that can perceive its environment, reason about it, and take actions to achieve goals
actionable: When designing agents, ensure they have perception (inputs), reasoning (LLM + logic), and actions (tool calls) as core components

claim: Agents differ from simple chain-of-thought because they can dynamically decide which tools to use based on the problem, not follow a predetermined sequence
actionable: Design agents with tool selection logic rather than hard-coded step sequences - use LLM reasoning to determine which tool to call next

claim: Agent loops run in cycles: perceive environment -> reason about it -> select action -> execute -> observe result -> repeat until goal is achieved
actionable: Implement agent loops with clear perception, reasoning, action selection, execution, and observation steps; ensure the loop can terminate when goals are met

claim: Tool use is central to agents - they call external tools and APIs to gather information, perform computations, or interact with systems
actionable: Design agents with a rich set of tools that allow them to perceive and act on their environment beyond just text generation

claim: Agent reasoning quality depends heavily on the quality of tool descriptions, examples, and the information returned from tools
actionable: Write clear, specific tool descriptions with examples; ensure tool outputs contain sufficient context for the agent to make informed decisions

claim: Reflection and error recovery are important - agents should reflect on failed actions and adjust their approach rather than repeating the same mistake
actionable: Implement error handling in agent loops that provides detailed error messages to the agent so it can adjust its next action accordingly

claim: Memory management in agents requires storing and retrieving relevant context efficiently to avoid context window overflows
actionable: Implement selective memory systems that retain relevant context while pruning irrelevant information to manage token usage in agent loops
